coredata.dc:title: Polymeric carbon quantum dots as efficient chlorophyll sensor-analysis based on experimental and computational investigation
coredata.dc:creator: Nazri, Nur Afifah Ahmad
Azeman, Nur Hidayah
Bakar, Mohd Hafiz Abu
Mobarak, Nadhratun Naiim
Masran, Athiyah Sakinah
Zain, Ahmad Rifqi Md
Mahdi, Mohd Adzir
Saputro, Adhitya Gandaryus
Wung, Triati Dewi Kencana
Luo, Yunhan
A. Bakar, Ahmad Ashrif
coredata.dc:description: This report introduces a microfiber chlorophyll sensor based on polymeric amine functionalized carbon quantum dots (NCQD) using the surface plasmon resonance technique. The silver (Ag) thin film, followed by NCQD-polyvinyl alcohol composite film, was deposited on the tapered optical fiber. The characterization of surface morphologies of NCQD-PVA film using a field emission scanning electron microscope shows that the contact area with the analyte was well covered with Ag and NCQD-PVA. The experimental results show that the fiber optic SPR sensor can detect chlorophyll in the 0.01–2.0 ppm range with a high sensitivity response and a detection limit of 1.90 nm ppm−1 and 0.78 ppm, respectively. This study observed no significant interference among the other ionic species (Fe3+, NH4 +, NO3 –, NO2 –, and PO4 -) due to the electrostatic and π-π interactions between NCQD and chlorophyll. Results from density functional theory calculation also confirm that the interaction of the NCQD with chlorophyll existed, and the strongest interaction occurs between nitrogen at pyridine sites of the carbon core and the oxygen group of chlorophyll. The proposed NCQD/PVA optical fiber sensor exhibits good sensing performance and correlates well with the standard method (R2 = 0.9501), suggesting a suitable technology candidate for real-time environmental monitoring applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Surface plasmon resonance
Chlorophyll
Optical sensor
Density functional theory

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of polyvinyl alcohol/carbon dots composite films
Fabrication of PVA/NCQD film on optical fiber
Construction of optical sensing setup for chlorophyll detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the Ministry of Higher Education (MOHE) , through the Fundamental Research Grant Scheme (FRGS) , under the grant number FRGS/1/2021/TK0/UKM/01/1.

The authors would like to thank the Photonics Technology Laboratory, Department of Electrical, Electronic and Systems Engineering, Faculty of Engineering and Built Environment, Institute of Microengineering and Nanoelectronics, and the Centre for Research and Instrumentation Management (CRIM), Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia for all amenities provided. Saputro, A.G. and Wungu, T.D.K. acknowledged the support provided by Institut Teknologi Bandung through the ‘Riset Unggulan ITB’ program.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication of Ag and carbon dots on optical fiber.
Experimental setup for detection of chlorophyll using LSPR setup.
XPS spectrum of NCQD of full spectrum (a), C1s (b), O1s (c), and N1s (d).
EDX analysis of the (a) Ag, (b) Ag/CQD-PVA, and (c) Ag/NCQD-PVA and mapping of the (d) Ag, (e) Ag/CQD-PVA, and (f) Ag/NCQD-PVA.
FESEM micrograph of the sensing materials films with cross-sectional image in the subset of (a) Ag, (b) Ag/CQD-PVA, and (c) Ag/NCQD-PVA.

The reflectance spectra of the LSPR sensor in the presence of chlorophyll solution with different of (a) Ag, (b) Ag/CQD-PVA, and (c) Ag/NCQD-PVA and (d) The wavelength shift values of chlorophyll concentration of fabrication probes.
The calibration plot of (blue) Ag, (black) Ag/CQD-PVA, and (red) Ag/NCQD-PVA.
(a) The repeatability of optical fiber sensor and (b) the selectivity of optical fiber sensor.
The response time of optical fiber of Ag/NCQD-PVA.
The Comparison of the chlorophyll concentration readings for optical fiber of Ag/NCQD-PVA sensors with the standard method.

The optimized geometric structures were composed of four different sites nitrogen (a) nitrogen in the basal (b) amide groups at the edge, (c) pyridinic nitrogen, and (d) pyrrolic nitrogen of graphitic carbon core of the carbon dots after interacting with chlorophyll.
The comparison performance of optical fiber sensors in this work.
The comparison performance of optical fiber sensor with other studies.
The comparison of adsorption distances and interaction energies of optimized geometric structures composed of four different sites nitrogen of carbon dots.

head.ce:title: Polymeric carbon quantum dots as efficient chlorophyll sensor-analysis based on experimental and computational investigation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Optical Fibre Sensing and Communications, College of Science and Engineering, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This report introduces a microfiber chlorophyll sensor based on polymeric amine functionalized carbon quantum dots (NCQD) using the surface plasmon resonance technique. The silver (Ag) thin film, followed by NCQD-polyvinyl alcohol composite film, was deposited on the tapered optical fiber. The characterization of surface morphologies of NCQD-PVA film using a field emission scanning electron microscope shows that the contact area with the analyte was well covered with Ag and NCQD-PVA. The experimental results show that the fiber optic SPR sensor can detect chlorophyll in the 0.01–2.0 ppm range with a high sensitivity response and a detection limit of 1.90 nm ppm−1 and 0.78 ppm, respectively. This study observed no significant interference among the other ionic species (Fe3+, NH4 +, NO3 –, NO2 –, and PO4 -) due to the electrostatic and π-π interactions between NCQD and chlorophyll. Results from density functional theory calculation also confirm that the interaction of the NCQD with chlorophyll existed, and the strongest interaction occurs between nitrogen at pyridine sites of the carbon core and the oxygen group of chlorophyll. The proposed NCQD/PVA optical fiber sensor exhibits good sensing performance and correlates well with the standard method (R2 = 0.9501), suggesting a suitable technology candidate for real-time environmental monitoring applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Phytoplankton consists of single-celled algae in marine ecosystems of various species and sizes [1]. However, the uncontrolled growth of phytoplankton can harm aquatic and human life. Phytoplankton, including cyanobacteria that accumulate in fish organs, affect the human kidney and liver because of cell damage through phosphate inhibition [2]. Consequently, measuring phytoplankton content in water is a priority in water quality assessment. Currently, the methods used to determine the phytoplankton in water samples include the conventional way [3] and remote monitoring [4]. The traditional spectrometric and chromatographic approaches are based on chlorophyll extraction, require extensive sample preparation, and are labor-intensive. Nowadays, some methods have been utilized for the real-time measurement of phytoplankton, e.g., remote sensing and portable fluorometers. These methods generally rely on visible and near-infrared spectral reflectance or fluorescence signal. However, the response of absorption measurement of phytoplankton or chlorophyll often overlaps and alters light intensity due to the presence of the total suspended solids and colored dissolved organic matter (DOM) [5]. The portable or in situ fluorometer continuously measures phytoplankton in the field. Generally, almost any fluorometer uses optical fiber to transmit light signals and a probe. Most optical fiber probes of fluorometer are microscale and single flat surface optical fiber. Although this fluorometer does not require pre-treatment or a large volume of samples, DOM in water samples became another drawback because of the noise from the sensor [6].

Recently, many optical fibers have been proposed to measure various analytes, such as heavy metals, enzymes, and biomolecules [7]. Numerous optical fibers were modified to improve the sensitivity and selectivity in sensing and measurement applications [8–10]. Therefore, the study of diverse structures of optical fiber sensors has grown, including tapered, fiber Bragg grating, D-shaped, and U-bent. The researchers have previously fabricated SPR-based microfiber with metal coated around the tapered region and show exceptional sensitivity enhancement for trace measurement of chemical analytes [11]. Generally, the SPR optical fiber sensor with a silver layer demonstrates higher sensitivity than the gold layer [12]. Moreover, many functionalized carbon materials have been working on microfiber sensors. Recently, Sangubotla et al. developed a laccase-immobilized microfiber-optic biosensor based on the fluorescence principle for detecting dopamine, resulting in excellent biocompatibility, sufficient stability, and good sensor performance [13].

Carbon dots are mainly used as a sensing platform in liquid form by monitoring the changes in their photoluminescence properties [14]. However, recent studies have proposed carbon quantum dots as sensing materials using the LSPR technique to measure chlorophyll [15]. A suitable matrix can immobilize carbon dots on a fiber surface. The nanocomposite of polymers such as polyvinyl alcohol, cellulose, chitosan, and polyethylene glycol with carbon dots allows them to be used as solid-phase materials [16]. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is one of the most popular polymers due to its easy preparation, atmospheric stability, and ability to alter its structure once mixed with carbon materials. The nanocomposite carbon dots/PVA has been successfully applied to catalysis, sensor, and food coloring applications [17–19]. For example, the solid-phase sensor for fluorescence detection has been developed by embedding carbon dots into PVA thin film trace Cr (VI) and ascorbic acid. Hence, the film forming of carbon dots using PVA polymer can be considered a potential material for optical fiber sensing applications.

Optical fiber sensors offered significant advantages in the development of real-time sensors. In this paper, we combined the advantages of SPR techniques and microfiber optics using carbon dots film as sensing materials to build a new optical sensor for chlorophyll detection. To achieve our purpose, we first synthesized functionalized carbon dots using the hydrothermal method, then loaded them onto PVA polymer to obtain (carbon dots-PVA) nanocomposite. Then, a thin silver film was deposited on the fiber through the chemical reduction method by reducing a metal ion salt solution. Subsequently, the carbon dots-PVA were fabricated on the fiber through the dip-coating method. FESEM and AFM characterized the thin films of carbon dots. After the SPR sensors were developed, the probe was applied to detect chlorophyll. The analytical performance characteristics of the proposed SPR microfiber sensors include the LOD, LOQ, linearity, range, sensitivity, selectivity, and repeatability. Finally, the proposed method was validated with the traditional method to determine the reliability of the sensor. To complement the experimental results for the sensing of chlorophyll, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were used to investigate the interaction between a single unit of NCQD of different nitrogen sites with chlorophyll.

Silver nitrate (99%), sodium hydroxide, ethanol, acetone, citric acid, polyethyleneimine (branched with an average molecular weight of 25 000) and Polyvinyl alcohol (average molecular weight of 31,000 to 50,000 Da, 87–89 % hydrolyzed) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Ethylenediamine, ammonia solution (30%), metal salts of monosodium phosphate, iron chloride, sodium nitrate and sodium nitrite were purchased from R&M Chemicals. Lastly, chlorophyll powder from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co Ltd.

The surface chemical composition and elemental analysis of amine functionalized carbon dots were measured using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Axis Ultra DLD Kratos/Shimadzu, Corporation of Kyoto, Japan). Surface morphology studies of optical fiber coated with Ag, Ag/CQD-PVA and Ag/NCQD-PVA were characterized using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (Bruker, MA, USA). The optical fibers were cut to measure the mapping, thickness, and cross-section of the samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of polyvinyl alcohol/carbon dots composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots were synthesized using the two-step hydrothermal method using citric acid, ethylenediamine, and polyethyleneimine based on the previous way [15]. The polymeric thin film dope solution was made as follows; 0.5 g of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water. The solution was stirred and heated to 70 °C. After the PVA solution was cooled to room temperature, 1 mL of CQD solution was poured into the PVA solution. The mixture is stirred until it forms a film-forming solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of PVA/NCQD film on optical fiber
ce:section.ce:para: Tapering was done using a Vytran glass processing machine (Vytran GPX-3400). Multi-mode optical fiber with 30 mm length and 40 mm tapered fiber probe from the original 62.5/125 mm. According to the published fabrication process in [9], 0.5 g of silver nitrate (AgNO3) was dissolved in 5 mL deionized water and stirred at 300 rpm. Next, a black precipitate formed after adding 10 mL of 2.0 M sodium hydroxide (NaOH) to the mixture, as shown in Fig. 1 . Then, 4.0 mL of 30% NH3 solution was added to the mix and stirred until no precipitate was observed. 2 g of commercial sugar dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water was added to the mixture to form the Ag mixture. The tip of the optical fiber is immersed in an Ag mixture at a temperature of 65 °C for 10 min to form an Ag layer. The Ag layer was dried in an oven at 50 °C for 5 min. The Ag layer was fully coated a second time on the tapered side using 0.125 g of AgNO3 using the same procedure. The film was coated twice at the end of the fiber to ensure the light was reflected. CQDs are coated on optical fibers using a dip coating method. The dip coating process begins with the optical fiber attached to the lifting arm of the lifting machine. Then, the suspended optical fiber was immersed in the film-forming solution for 30 s. The dipping and pulling speeds were at 65 mm/min, dried at 50 °C using an oven for 5 min, and repeated five times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of optical sensing setup for chlorophyll detection
ce:section.ce:para: A schematic diagram of the sensing setup is shown in Fig. 2 . The components of the optical fiber sensing setup consist of an Ocean Optics laser (DH-2000-BAL), an Ocean Optics spectrometer (HR4000CG-UV-NIR) with a wavelength detection range between 200 and 1100 nm, a two-way coupler (2 × 2), 30 mm length of 40 μm tapered multimode optical fiber probe, computer and tapered optical fiber probe coated with sensing material. Light from the laser is coupled into fiber one and propagates through a bidirectional coupler to the fiber optic probe (fiber 2). Then, the light is reflected from the end face of fiber 2, bringing back the SPR signal. Further, the end of the signal propagating through the coupler is measured by the spectrometer and processed by a computer to display the reflection spectrum. SpectraSuite software was used to analyze the spectra recorded by the Ocean Optics spectrometer. A range of 0.01–8 ppm chlorophyll solution was used as a test sample. The test sample was dipped in the prepared chlorophyll analyte solution and analyzed using SPR measurements.

DFT calculations were carried out to study the interaction between nitrogen sites in functionalized carbon dots and chlorophyll. The geometry optimizations of the functionalized carbon dots and chlorophyll were performed using the Gaussian 09 package. B3LYP hybrid functional is used as the exchange–correlation functional, and the 6-31G basis set is used to expand the atomic orbitals. The model structures were composed of four different sites of nitrogen: amide groups at the edge, pyrrolic nitrogen, pyridinic nitrogen, and nitrogen in the basal of graphitic carbon dots. The DFT method was used to analyze the interaction between carbon dots functionalized with chlorophyll as the analyte for the sensor. These model structures of carbon dots have demonstrated a good approximation for the interaction of molecules on chlorophyll.

A standard reference of chlorophyll standard solution (chlorophyll powder from Tokyo Chemical) was prepared to evaluate the instrument's performance in measuring chlorophyll [20]. The absolute quantity or concentration range of the instrument responding to an analyte is the linear detection range, and the minimal linearity defines the detection limit. In this work, the primary dilution of chlorophyll standard solution was used to create a series of chlorophyll calibration standards ranging from 0.01 to 10 ppm.

The surface elements of functionalized carbon dots were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the full spectrum of carbon dots was represented by the C1s peak at 282 eV, the O1s peak at 527 eV, and the N1s peak at 396 eV. Furthermore, C1s peak (Fig. 3(b)) can be deconvoluted into three different surface components, which are graphitic carbon or sp2 of NCN (281.5 eV) [21], CC (282.4 eV) and CC (283.0 eV) [22]. The O1s peak (Fig. 3(c)) can be decomposed into two peaks at 395.7 eV and 397.0 eV, which should correspond to the nitride-like species or aromatic N-imines and C-N, respectively [23,24]. In Fig. 3(d), the peak of O1s is also divided into three components: aromatic carbon atoms containing some functional group such as carbonyl, ketone, or a carboxylic compound formed during carbonization (527.4 eV) [25], CO/COC (528.1 eV) [26] and CO (528.7 eV) [27]. The results of XPS show the existence of amine groups and graphitic structures on their surfaces.

The morphology of fabricated fibers can be analyzed using FESEM and AFM characterization. The chemical composition of the Ag, Ag/CQD-PVA and Ag/NCQD-PVA film was evaluated from EDX data, and the distribution was recorded by mapping (Fig. 4 ). EDX analysis confirms the presence of silver and carbon on the fiber. The analysis results show that the mass percentage and ratio (Wt) of silver atoms (Ag) decreased when the carbon dots were coated on the fiber surface with 84 % (Ag) to 77 % (Ag/NCQD-PVA). In parallel with the results of the carbon atom analysis, the percentage of this atom increased from 4.9 % on Ag fiber to 7.6 % on Ag/CQD-PVA fiber and further increased to 10.3 % on Ag/NCQD-PVA fiber. No additional elements were detected on the fiber of carbon dots and PVA nanocomposite; the low percentage of Si element demonstrates its excellent purity and well coating.

To achieve proper signal reflection in SPR-based sensors, it's crucial to deposit a metal layer on the optical fiber with a thickness of at least 200–300 nm [28]. Fig. 5 reveals the FESEM views of the surface and cross-sectional area of the fibers. By comparing all the cross-sectional images, it is found that the film of the Ag layer has thickness ranges from 230 to 400 nm. The addition of Ag/CQD-PVA and Ag/NCQD-PVA nanocomposite film on the fiber becomes thick in the range of about 300 to 600 nm, as shown in the subset of Fig. 5 (b) and 5 (c), respectively. The silver layer is well coated in the observed field on the 200–400 nm layer range in a subset of Fig. 5 (a). However, based on the image analysis, some agglomeration of silver was observed. This agglomeration occurs during the chemical process of reduction of silver salts during the synthesis of silver nanoparticles. Bulk-state silver nanoparticles form in two steps: nucleation and subsequent particle growth. During the procedure, the coagulation of growth of the nanoparticles is due to the large number of nucleation sites leading to non-linear thickness on the surface of the fiber [29]. The nucleation rate is also susceptible to temperature variations [30]. However, in this work, the temperature was kept constant at 65 °C by monitoring the temperature regularly using a thermometer. Fig. 5 (b) & 5 (c) shows the surface morphology of Ag/carbon dots-polyvinyl alcohol (Ag/CQD-PVA) composite film and Ag/carbon dots-polyvinyl alcohol (Ag/NCQD-PVA) composite film, respectively. Carbon dots are distributed uniformly in the PVA matrix. However, it shows that Ag/NCQD-PVA film seems to be a larger agglomerate than Ag/CQD-PVA film. This agglomerated because of the increase in the loading of NCQD in the system. The graphitic structures of NCQD tend to agglomerate in the film process layer by layer because of their excellent interplanar interactions [31].

After the fabrication of sensing materials onto fibers, the probe response toward chlorophyll solution was tested at different concentrations. All experiments were carried out at room temperature. All the tapered fibers were immersed in chlorophyll solutions at a particular time. The reflectance spectrum of SPR fiber was monitored during this process in the wavelength of 380 nm–400 nm. However, noisyreflection spectra observed during the experiment became one of the disadvantages of this proposed method. The main reasons for the noise were the lower intensity of the detected scattered light from the single nanoparticle as well as the thickness and uneven distribution of sensing materials coating layers as shown in FESEM and EDX analysis above [32]. Fortunately, in this experiment, such errors do not affect the curvature as long as the overall peak shape remains unchanged [33]. All complete reflection spectrums of the fabricated sensors were given in Supplementary Materials S1. The spectral change of the sensor is shown in Fig. 6 (a-c). As shown in the spectrum, with increased chlorophyll concentrations, the reflection wavelength gradually moved toward a shorter/longer wavelength, showing the interaction of chlorophyll and sensing materials (Fig. 6(d)). The SPR reflectance peak of Ag shows a slight change in wavelength shift at the chlorophyll concentration; meanwhile, the reflectance peak of Ag/CQD-PVA shows a larger difference in wavelength shift than Ag within a few minutes. Furthermore, the wavelength of the Ag/NCQD-PVA reflectance spectrum has a red shift with significant change and finally stopped after reaching 6 ppm. These results show that the sensing materials of carbon dots were able to sensitively detect the chlorophyll through electrostatic interactions between cationic sensing materials and anionic chlorophyll [15].

The performance of the optical fibers sensor was evaluated in terms of the limit of detection (LOD), the limit of quantification (LOQ), response time, linearity, range, sensitivity, selectivity, and repeatability. Fig. 7 shows the relationship between the wavelength shift (Δλ) and the concentration of chlorophyll (0.01–8 ppm). It was observed that the linear response is in the range of 0.01–2 ppm (R2 = 0.9602) and 2–6 ppm (R2 = 9852) with sensitivities of 1.90 nm/ppm and 0.33 nm/ppm, respectively, for Ag/NCQD-PVA. The observed linear range for the sensor thin film at 0.01–2 ppm, Ag/NCQD-PVA displays a better correlation than Ag (R2 = 0.343) and Ag/CQD-PVA (R2 = 0.6871). LOD values may be described as LOD = 3S/b, where LOQ can be expressed as LOQ = 10S/b. S is the response's standard deviation, which can be estimated from the standard deviation of a regression line's y-intercept. The LODs were determined as 0.78 ppm, 3.50 ppm, and 5.37 ppm for Ag/NCQD-PVA, Ag/CQD-PVA, and Ag fiber sensors, respectively. Meanwhile, the LOQs were determined as 2.63 ppm, 11.56 ppm, and 17.98 ppm for Ag/NCQD-PVA, Ag/CQD-PVA, and Ag fiber sensors, respectively. In summary, the optical fiber of SPR Ag/NCQD-PVA proclaims exceptional performance compared to Ag/CQD-PVA and Ag in terms of sensitivity, linearity, LOD and LOQ. Table 1 shows the comparison of fiber performance in this work for chlorophyll detection methods.

The performance of the optical fiber sensor in terms of response time, selectivity, and repeatability was examined at a 1 ppm concentration of chlorophyll. Each measurement was repeated five times on each fabricated sensor probe. The repeatability measurement of optical fiber sensor is shown in Fig. 8 (a). The larger wavelength shifts by the Ag/NCQD-PVA optical fiber compared to the Ag/CQD-PVA optical fiber indicates a better sensitivity of this optical fiber sensor to detect chlorophyll. However, the significant wavelength shift error in the five experiments observed may be due to the unchanging surrounding environment, such as the ambient temperature, and the error when the experiment was carried out could affect the experimental results [34]. The selectivity of the fiber towards chlorophyll was compared by monitoring the reflectance peak variations of the fiber with potential interference, such as Fe3+, NH4 +, NO3 –, NO2 –, and PO4 - as shown in Fig. 8 (b) at one ppm of concentrations. It was observed that the fiber reacted slightly with other interference, suggesting that the fiber probe was selective towards chlorophyll detection (380 nm – 390 nm). Thus, this study has indicated consistent findings with the previous technique of LSPR using functionalized CQD and triangle silver nanoparticles to detect chlorophyll by Nazri et al. [15]. This is due to the electrostatic interaction between amino groups of NCQD and oxygen groups of chlorophyll. The addition of amine groups proved that functionalized carbon dots enhanced the performance of fiber optic sensors. Functional groups of the amine on the surface and edges of carbon dots serve as active sites for interactions with chlorophyll. However, other metal ions can still interact with NCQD, causing the increase in wavelength shift after all ion has been mixed with all interfering ions solutions [9]. From the previous report, NCQD exhibited a strong band of absorption of 238 nm corresponding to π-π * transition (CC), and XPS analysis indicated the presence of CC/CO groups and graphitic structures. In this study, the interaction between the aromatic core of NCQD and chlorophyll solution induces the π-π stacking due to affinity of NCQD with chlorophyll, which also tends to be a mechanism interaction [35].

The response time of the sensors was examined as shown in Fig. 9 . The Ag/CQD-PVA optical fiber recorded a period of 300 s (5 min) to detect chlorophyll stably while the Ag/NCQD-PVA optical fiber took 420 s (7 min). The reaction time of the optical sensor of Ag has a stability response in a short time compared to the Ag/CQD-PVA and Ag/NCQD-PVA. This is because of the properties of PVA polymer that are added to carbon dots to produce the film-forming solutions. Hydroxyl groups of PVA reacted with the hydroxyl groups of carbon dots, forming hydrogen bonding, consequently making the film insoluble in water. However, hydroxyl groups can absorb water and inevitably swell the film [19]. This phenomenon causes the Ag/CQD-PVA and Ag/NCQD-PVA optical fiber to require longer stable response times than Ag, as shown in Fig. 9. Hence, fiber optic Ag/NCQD-PVA exhibited good performance compared to fiber optic Ag and Ag/CQD-PVA. Table 2 compares the proposed sensor's performance with the formerly reported sensor for chlorophyll sensing application. In conclusion, the proposed sensor from this study exhibited a comparable performance with other studies. Because carbon dots and PVA polymer are sensitive and selective toward chlorophyll, fiber optics are a very flexible analytical support tool. This proposed fiber optic sensor system offers a lot of analytical potential.

The ability of Ag/NCQD-PVA fiber sensor was compared to the spectrophotometry standard method to determine the potency of fabricated sensors in measuring chlorophyll concentrations. The functionality of the proposed fiber optic sensor was determined by calculating the correlation coefficient (R2) using the same detection of chlorophyll content of standard solution as the standard spectrometer technique. Both techniques detected chlorophyll in five independent replicates of eight different concentrations in the range of 0.03 ppm to 5 ppm. Afterwards, the chlorophyll measurements were analyzed, as shown in Fig. 10 . Fig. 10 shows the comparison of chlorophyll concentration measurements for Ag/NCQD-PVA fiber sensor (y-axis) with the spectrometry standard method (x-axis). The optical fiber sensor and spectrometry approaches produced satisfactory linear regressions with correlation coefficients of 0.9501, implying that they were statistically similar. In conclusion, we can state that the spectrometry system effectively validated the accuracy and precision of particular binding investigations using the microfiber SPR sensor approach.

The single NCQD unit of sensing film has four possible N-sites that can interact with the O group of the chlorophyll molecule. The optimized geometric structures of free NCQD (without analyte) and the possible corresponding N sites of carbon dots with analyte chlorophyll are shown in Fig. 11 . The calculated interaction energies (eV) and the selected parameters of the bond lengths (Å) of oxygen and nitrogen for four different sites between the NCQD unit and chlorophyll are summarized in Table 3 . The interaction energies for amide at the edge of the carbon core NCQD with chlorophyll is −2.72 eV meanwhile, nitrogen at the basal of carbon core NCQD with chlorophyll is −4.74 eV. Furthermore, the interaction energies of nitrogen in pyrrolic and pyridinic sites of the carbon core of NCQD with chlorophyll are −3.85 and −5.06 eV, respectively. The DFT that calculated interactions energies suggested that interaction energies of nitrogen in the pyridinic site of the carbon core of NCQD are preferred compared to the nitrogen at the edge, basal and pyrrolic sites of the carbon core NCQD. The DFT calculation results indicate that the interaction energy of a single NCQD unit with chlorophyll occurred, which helps to explain why the addition of nitrogen into the surface of carbon dots enhanced the performance of the optical fiber sensor.

An optical microfiber sensor for chlorophyll in the solution phase based on functionalized carbon dots was obtained. Further, the Ag and carbon dots film was immobilized on the silver-coated tapered optical fiber using a redox reaction and simple dip-coating process. EDX analysis confirmed the existence of Ag and carbon with a low percentage of Si components on the fiber. In addition, the FESEM analysis image showed the tapered fiber's contact area covered with some of the accumulations of Ag due to the nucleation process and temperature variations. Furthermore, the tapered optical fiber of Ag/NCQD-PVA exhibited excellent performance compared to Ag and Ag/CQD-PVA with a high sensitivity response of 1.90 ppm nm−1, a detection limit of 0.78 ppm for the chlorophyll concentration ranging from 0.01 to 2.0 ppm also no significant interference among the other ionic species (Fe3+, NH4 +, NO3 –, NO2 –, and PO4 -). The wavelength red-shift in the sensor was the graphitic structure and existence of amine groups that cause the electrostatic and π-π interactions between NCQD and chlorophyll. The DFT computational analyses strongly support our experimentally obtained results that the interaction between the NCQD and chlorophyll occurred between the nitrogen of carbon dots and oxygen groups of chlorophyll. The proposed optical fiber-based SPR and NCQD-PVA composite film offers excellent performance compared to the previous LSPR-based sensor on glass substrates with good correlation with the standard method (R2 = 0.9501). Finally, this work may have a wide range of potential applications for environmental monitoring.
This research was funded by the Ministry of Higher Education (MOHE), through the Fundamental Research Grant Scheme (FRGS), under the grant number FRGS/1/2021/TK0/UKM/01/1.

Nur Afifah Ahmad Nazri: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Nur Hidayah Azeman: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Mohd Hafiz Abu Bakar: Writing – review & editing. Nadhratun Naiim Mobarak: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Athiyah Sakinah Masran: . Ahmad Rifqi Md Zain: . Mohd Adzir Mahdi: . Adhitya Gandaryus Saputro: . Triati Dewi Kencana Wung: . Yunhan Luo: Writing – review & editing. Ahmad Ashrif A. Bakar: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Photonics Technology Laboratory, Department of Electrical, Electronic and Systems Engineering, Faculty of Engineering and Built Environment, Institute of Microengineering and Nanoelectronics, and the Centre for Research and Instrumentation Management (CRIM), Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia for all amenities provided. Saputro, A.G. and Wungu, T.D.K. acknowledged the support provided by Institut Teknologi Bandung through the ‘Riset Unggulan ITB’ program.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optlastec.2023.110259.

ce:section.ce:para:
